questionBank.question: If y=(3x^2+1)^5, then y'=
questionBank.explanation: Chain rule: outer derivative times inner derivative 6x.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Differentiate outside, keep inside, multiply by inside derivative.

questionBank.question: If x^2+y^2=25, then dy/dx=
questionBank.notesSnippet: Treat y as y(x), so derivative of y^2 is 2y y'.

questionBank.question: If f and f^{-1} are inverses and f(2)=5, f'(2)=4, then (f^{-1})'(5)=
questionBank.notesSnippet: Swap input/output point, then reciprocal slope.
questionBank.timeTip: Match the point carefully before reciprocal.

questionBank.question: d/dx[arctan x] =
questionBank.explanation: Standard derivative formula for arctan x.
questionBank.timeTip: This is a common AP quick point.

questionBank.question: If f(x)=x^3, then f''(x)=
questionBank.notesSnippet: Second derivative is derivative of first derivative.

questionBank.question: If 3x^2−2y+4xy=6, then dy/dx =
questionBank.explanation: Implicit differentiation gives 6x − 2y′ + 4y + 4xy′ = 0, so y′(4x−2) = −6x−4y and y′ = (−6x−4y)/(4x−2) = (6x+4y)/(2−4x), equivalent to (3x+2y)/(1−2x) (mixed practice #21).
questionBank.notesSnippet: Differentiate y terms with dy/dx; collect y′ terms.
questionBank.timeTip: After differentiating, factor y′ on one side.

questionBank.question: If F(x)=(x^2+1)^3, then F′(1) =
questionBank.explanation: F′(x)=3(x^2+1)^2·2x; F′(1)=3(2)^2·2=24 (chain rule drill matching mixed derivative packet style).
questionBank.timeTip: Evaluate the inner function first, then multiply by its derivative.

questionBank.question: Which identity is used when differentiating y = arcsin(x)?
questionBank.explanation: d/dx arcsin(x)=1/√(1−x^2) on (−1,1) (standard inverse trig rule from derivative packets).
questionBank.timeTip: Memorize arcsin, arctan, and arccos derivatives.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find dy/dx in terms of x and y.
steps: Differentiate both sides: 2xy + x^2y' + 3y^2y' = 0.
Group y' terms: (x^2+3y^2)y' = -2xy.
Solve for y'.
parts.scoring: 1 pt implicit setup, 1 pt combine, 1 pt isolate, 1 pt final.

frqBank.answerKeyPdf: Unit 3/Homework /3-5 & 3-6 WK AB Derivative Procedures and Higher Order Derivatives #2 ANSWERS.pdf

3.1.title: 3-1 Chain Rule
3.1.content:
FUN-3 · Composite functions

Chain rule (core pattern)

If y = f(u) and u = g(x), then y is a composite of f and g. The outer function’s derivative is evaluated at the inner function, times the inner’s derivative.

d/dx[f(g(x))] = f′(g(x)) · g′(x)

How to apply it

  1. Identify the outer function (last operation you would undo).
  2. Differentiate the outer, leave the inner unchanged inside.
  3. Multiply by the derivative of the inner.
  4. Repeat if nested (layers of chain).
AP tip: For eu, ln u, sin u, etc., always multiply by u′.

3.2.title: 3-2 Implicit Differentiation
3.2.content:
FUN-3 · Implicit relations

When y is not solved for

If x and y satisfy an equation like x² + y² = 25 or xy = sin(x+y), differentiate both sides with respect to x, treating y as y(x).

Every y-term brings a y′

d/dx[y²] = 2y · y′  ·  d/dx[xy] = y + xy′ (product rule)

Then collect terms with y′ on one side and solve for y′.

AP tip: At a point (x₀, y₀), plug in after solving for y′ to get the numerical slope.

3.3.title: 3-3 to 3-5 Inverse + Higher Derivatives
3.3.content:
FUN-3 · Inverses

Derivative of an inverse function

If f is one-to-one and differentiable, and f(a) = b, then

(f⁻¹)′(b) = 1 / f′(a)   provided f′(a) ≠ 0

Slope of inverse at b is reciprocal of slope of f at the matching a.

Inverse trig derivatives

Know d/dx[arcsin x], arccos x, arctan x — they come from implicit diff on sin y = x, etc.

Higher-order derivatives

f″, f‴ mean repeat differentiation. For notation, y″ = d/dx(y′). Context (motion, concavity) often names f″ as acceleration or concavity driver.

AP tip: Simplify y′ before differentiating again to avoid algebra mistakes.

referenceHtml:
Unit 3 — Materials on this site include 5 notes PDFs, 16 homework PDFs, and 1 review PDFs. Copy your course PDFs into the project's materials/ folder, keeping paths like Unit 2/Homework/… so FRQ iframes and Packet PDF links load.

The formula blocks below are the shared AP Calc AB reference; your unit packets explain how they show up in your course.
referenceBlurb: Shared AB formulas plus a short pointer to this unit’s PDF materials.
